coredata.dc:title: Dual-template molecularly surface imprinted polymer on fluorescent metal-organic frameworks functionalized with carbon dots for ascorbic acid and uric acid detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Pirot, Shano M.
Omer, Khalid M.
Alshatteri, Azad H.
Ali, Gona K.
Shatery, Omer B.A.
coredata.dc:description: In this work, dual-template molecularly imprinted polymer surfaces imprinted on blue fluorescent Cr-based MOF (Cr-MOF) functionalized with yellow emissive carbon dots (Y-CDs) were prepared using l-ascorbic acid (AA) and uric acid (UA) as templates for simultaneous selective recognition of AA and UA. The as-prepared nanocomposite probe (Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP) contains two recognition site cavities and emits a dual well-resolved fluorescence spectra when excited at 390 nm; blue emission (λem 450 nm) is due to Cr-MOF, and yellow emission (λem 560 nm) is due to Y-CDs. The yellow fluorescence emission of Y-CDs was quenched upon the addition of ascorbic acid, while Cr-MOF’s emission remained unaffected. In the same way, the blue fluorescence emission of the Cr-MOFs was quenched in the presence of uric acid, while the yellow emission remained constant. Both emissions were quenched in a sample containing both AA and UA. This can be exploited to design a dual-template biosensor to detect UA and AA simultaneously. The Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP sensor displayed a dynamic linear response for AA in the range 25.0 µM – 425.0 µM with a detection limit of 1.30 µM, and for UA in the range 25.0 µM – 425.0 µM with a detection limit of 1.10 µM. The dual-target probe Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP was highly selective and sensitive for the detection of UA and AA in human urine samples due to the selectivity of the two recognition sites.

head.ce:title: Dual-template molecularly surface imprinted polymer on fluorescent metal-organic frameworks functionalized with carbon dots for ascorbic acid and uric acid detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, dual-template molecularly imprinted polymer surfaces imprinted on blue fluorescent Cr-based MOF (Cr-MOF) functionalized with yellow emissive carbon dots (Y-CDs) were prepared using l-ascorbic acid (AA) and uric acid (UA) as templates for simultaneous selective recognition of AA and UA. The as-prepared nanocomposite probe (Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP) contains two recognition site cavities and emits a dual well-resolved fluorescence spectra when excited at 390 nm; blue emission (λem 450 nm) is due to Cr-MOF, and yellow emission (λem 560 nm) is due to Y-CDs. The yellow fluorescence emission of Y-CDs was quenched upon the addition of ascorbic acid, while Cr-MOF’s emission remained unaffected. In the same way, the blue fluorescence emission of the Cr-MOFs was quenched in the presence of uric acid, while the yellow emission remained constant. Both emissions were quenched in a sample containing both AA and UA. This can be exploited to design a dual-template biosensor to detect UA and AA simultaneously. The Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP sensor displayed a dynamic linear response for AA in the range 25.0 µM – 425.0 µM with a detection limit of 1.30 µM, and for UA in the range 25.0 µM – 425.0 µM with a detection limit of 1.10 µM. The dual-target probe Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP was highly selective and sensitive for the detection of UA and AA in human urine samples due to the selectivity of the two recognition sites.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Detection of ascorbic acid (AA) and uric acid (UA) is highly crucial in biomedical sciences due to their significant roles in various human metabolisms [1–7]. AA, also known as vitamin C, is a water-soluble vitamin found in many biological systems, food, and multivitamin supplements. In the body, it acts as an antioxidant, works against free radicals to protect cells from the damage. It has been used to prevent and treat common colds, mental disease, infertility, cancer, and some HIV-related clinical symptoms[8–14]. UA is the end-product of purine nucleotide metabolism and its derivatives. High concentrations of UA in body fluids are associated with several medical conditions, such as gout, kidney diseases, hyperuricemia, Lesch–Nyhansyndrome, and cardiovascular disease [15–22].

The simultaneous detection of UA and AA is needed for clinical diagnosis because they are coexistent in biological fluids, such as serum, blood, and urine, and vitamin C intake is inversely associated with serum uric acid concentrations [23]. Therefore, it is important to develop an assayto detect UA and AA simultaneously with high selectivity and sensitivity in routine assay [24]. Various methods were used for the single detection of UA and AA, such as enzyme-based methods [25–27], electrochemical methods [28–30], high performance liquid chromatography [31–33], spectrophotometry [34–38], and chemiluminescence analysis [39–41]. Moreover, based on the literature, very few methods were used for simultaneous detection of UA and AA, and most were based on an electrochemical sensor [4,7] chemiluminescence [42], spectrophotometry [43], HPLC-UV [44], and HPLC-EDS [45]. However, these techniques have some disadvantages and problems, such as complexity, cost, low sensitivity, and selectivity, especially in the biological fluid matrix. Thus, it is highly important to develop a biochemical probe with many merits, such as high selectivity, sensitivity, low cost, stability, reusability, and ease of use.

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have received significant attention as an interesting tool for creating custom molecular recognition elements. [46–51] They mimic antibody-like recognition in nature by synthesizing polymers with cavities based on template molecules as recognition sites. MIPs are superior to natural antibodies as artificial identifying systems because they are inexpensive, long-lasting, and abrasion resistant. In general, the stages involved in the production of MIPs include self-assembly of template and functional monomers, polymerization triggered by an initiator and a cross-linker, and extraction of template molecules to produce specific recognition sites [52–60].

Various methods are used for the preparation of MIPs, such as bulk polymerization, emulsion polymerization, suspension polymerization, precipitation polymerization, and sol–gel polymerization. The sol–gel method is a simple and efficient method for preparing MIPs and can overcome some disadvantages of common MIP preparation methods, such as swelling, low binding capacity, and non-specific binding [61].

According to the type of interactions between target molecules and functional monomers in the pre–polymerization mixture and during rebinding, MIPs can be synthesized via a number of various techniques, like covalent, non–covalent, semi–covalent, or ion imprinting interactions [62].

Currently, the majority of MIPs are made from a single template and only contain one sort of recognition site for one particular target molecule, making it impossible for them to have high affinity and selectivity for a group of targets. For many applications with complex matrices, one analyte detection at a time is not practical [63–70]. MIPs have been designed using two or more templates to improve usability and broaden application possibilities[71,72]. Therefore, it is crucial to use dual-type cavities when designing a single MIP. One should consider the selection of suitable probes for this purpose in a design with such high standards.

MIPs have been used as sensing elements in bio/chemosensor as a recognizer since the late 1990 s. Also, miniaturized sensors play a significant role in the world of nanotechnology.The current trend of sensors is towards miniaturization because they are smaller, faster, thinner, and more reliable for point-of-care diagnostics [72–74].

Nanomaterial fluorescent probes based on metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) are attracting numerous studies due to their controllable pore sizes, excellent encapsulation ability, large specific surface area, predictable nanostructures, and hierarchical pores [75–77]. Many fluorescence sensors based on fluorescent MOFs probes have been proposed for various applications. Although fluorescent MOF probes have seen significant success in a variety of applications, their large size and unstable structures pose difficulties in fluorescence detection, such as poor stability and sensitivity. To prepare stable and efficient fluorescent probes, it is critical to control the size and morphology of the fluorescence MOFs [78–85]. For instance, strong fluorescent carbon dots@MOFs were constructed for enhanced sensing [86]. Additionally, using carbon dots with MOFs gained more attention because of their unique optical properties, excellent biocompatibility, good water solubility, and convenient surface modification flexibility [87–94].

Recently, hybridized surface imprinting of MIP on MOFs has been developed to synergistically combine the characteristics of both platforms, such as numerous cavities for selective recognition and large specific surface areas for effective adsorption. Additionally, the MIP@MOF composite showed improved properties such as excellent thermal stability, uniform morphology, and homogeneous polymer film, which offers a faster mass transfer rate than the bulk MIP [95–97]. Therefore, through the development of hybridized surface imprinting of MIP@MOFs, the beneficial properties of both materials' particular molecular recognition sites for selective adsorption and many pores with high specific surface areas for effective adsorption have recently been synergistically coupled [95–97].

In the present study, the preparation of a dual-template MIP on a functionalized emissive MOF for the detection of AA and UA is described. When the probe is excited with λex = 390 nm, the Y-CDs (λem = 560 nm) have a response to AA, and blue Cr-MOF (λem = 450 nm) have a response to UA. Moreover, in the presence of the two targets together, the emission of both emitters is quenched. MIP@MOF can enhance both the selectivity and reliability of the ratiometric probe. Fig. 1 shows a schematic diagram for the preparation and application of the Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP.

All the reagents used were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), Chromium Nitrate nonahydrate Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, 3-Aminoterephthalic acid (NH2-H2BDC), tetaethylorthosilicate (TEOS, % 99.0), (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES, % 98.0), ammonium solution (25 % NH4·H2O), ascorbic acid, O-phenylenediamine (OPD), Uric acid (UA), Ascorbic acid (AA), and methanol purchased from commercial suppliers (Merck and Sigma-Aldrich),.

A Cary 60 Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA) was used to record UV–Vis absorption spectra.Fluorescence spectrawere recorded via a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA), both the emission and excitation slits were set at 5.0 nm. FTIR spectra were recorded using a Varian 640 FTIR spectrophotometer (Palo Alto, CA, USA). Anempyrean X-ray diffractometer (PANalytical, Netherland) was used to collect the X-ray diffraction patterns. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Zeiss-EM10C (100 KV, German) was used to characterize the morphology and particle size. A FE-SEM (field emissionscanning electron microscopy), EDS and elemental mapping was characterized by (Zeiss sigma 300).

Y-CDs were prepared according to the previous work with slight modifications [98]. Briefly, 0.0184 M of OPD was dissolved in 25 mL of water and transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave, followed by heating at 180 °C in an oven for 14 h. After cooling at room temperature, the resultant mixture was centrifuged and dialyzed for 48 h to obtain Y-CDs.

Cr-MOF (MIL-101(Cr)–NH2) was synthesized according to previous work [99]. A mixture of 1.6 g (0.2 M) Cr (NO3)3·9(H2O), 0.72 g (0.1 M) 2-aminoterephthalic acid, and 0.4 g (0.3 M) sodium hydroxide was dispersed in 30 mL deionized water and then sonicated for 30 min. The resulting solution was heated in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 150 °C for 12 h. After cooling the reaction mixture to room temperature, the green solid was collected and washed with deionized water and methanol. The product was dried at 80 °C in an oven.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of dual templates of Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP with dual templates was prepared based on reports published in the literature with some modification [65,66,100]. In brief, 10 mg of Cr-MOF was dispersed in 10 mL of deionized water, mixed with 5.0 mL of Y-CDs, and stirred for 5 min. Then, a pre-polymerization consisting of 10 mg (0.057 mmol) of AA and 10 mg (0.059 mmol) of UA with 100 µL of APTES (monomer) was added to the above mixture and stirred for 2 h. Next, 300 µL of TEOS (cross-linking agent) and 300 µL of NH3·H2O were added to the solution. The mixture was kept at room temperature in a stirred container for 16 h. Finally, the solid product was centrifuged, washed, and dried. The templates of AA and UA were removed by washing with a mixture of methanol, deionized water, and ethanol(1:1:3, v/v). For comparison, the nonimprinted polymers (Y-CDs/Cr-MOF @NIPs) were also constructed using the same method but without addition of AA and UA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of AA and UA
ce:section.ce:para: For the first condition, AA and UA separately, the fluorescence spectra were recorded with an excitation wavelength of 390 nm for the two targets. For the second condition, an AA/UA mixture, the fluorescence spectra were recorded by mixing the two targets in the same solution. In a typical procedure, 2.0 mL of the probe Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP suspension was added to a 5 mL volumetric flask. Next, different concentrations of AA and UA solutions were added, and the volume was diluted to 5 mL with buffer.

Urine samples were obtained from normal volunteers. A volume of 150 µL of each urine sample was spiked by adding different concentrations of the standard ascorbic acid and uric acid solutions, 150.0, 250.0, and 350.0 µM, respectively. Next, the mixture was diluted with deionized water after the addition of 1.0 mL of our probe Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP.

In order to simultaneously determine UA and AA, Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP should be excited at the same excitation wavelength (λex = 390 nm), and their fluorescence signals should be monitored at the same time without mutual interference.

As shown in Fig. 1A, both probes, Cr-MOF and Y-CDs, were synthesized using the hydrothermal method. Fig. 1B displays the synthesis of the MIP@MOF by sol–gel polymerizations. In this work, we used non-covalent interaction as an imprinting strategy. The interaction of the hydroxyl portion of AA and the amino portion of UA with the amino group of APTES supported the specific recognition of the Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP towards AA and UA.During the entrapment of both templates, UA and AA, the emission of both emitters, Cr-MOF and Y-CDs, was quenched, indicating the presence of both templates. After ascorbic acid and uric acid was removed by washing for several time (methanol: ethanol: H2O, 1:1:3) the empty imprinted cavities were obtained for the two targets in the Y-CDs/Cr-MOF @MIP. Hence, the fluorescence emissions of both emitters were restored. Fig. 1C shows a response signal for the probe Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP. The blue emissive, Cr-MOF (λem = 450 nm), responded to UA, while Y-CDs (λem = 560 nm) responded to AA. By adding AA alone, the emission of Y-CDs was quenched, and the emission color became pure blue, attributed to the emission of Cr-MOF. While adding UA, the blue emission was quenched and the yellow emission color disappeared due to the presence of Y-CDs.

The fluorescence spectra of Cr-MOF, Y-CDs, and Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP were recorded as shown in Fig. 2 A. The Cr-MOF nanocomposite showed a fluorescence spectrum peaked at 450 nm upon excitation at 390 nm (Fig. 2A, curve a). The Cr-MOF also showed intense, bright blue luminescence under UV irradiation at 365 nm. Meanwhile, the yellow fluorescence of Y-CDs showed a fluorescence maximum at 560 nm (Fig. 2A, curve b). After the two fluorescence emitters, Y-CDs and Cr-MOF, were incorporated into the MIP, two well-resolved emission peaks at 450 nm and 560 nm (Fig. 2A, curve d) were recorded under excitation at 390 nm. The emission spectra of Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@NIP were recorded as shown in Fig. 2A, curve c. The Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP emission displays pale yellow fluorescence under UV irradiation at 365 nm. The UV–Vis spectra of Y-CDs, Cr-MOF, and Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP were recorded and shown in Fig. 2B. Excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra were recorded, as shown in Fig. S1. It is obvious that the product is excitation-dependent; λex = 390 nm was selected as the best excitation wavelength for the two emitters in the ratiometric design.

The functional groups of Cr-MOF, Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP, and Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@NIP were characterized by Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. Fig. 2C shows the typical broad band in 3600∼2500 cm−1, two peaks at 3454 cm−1 and 3140 cm−1, and the peaks in 1369∼1515 cm−1, which were attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH in carboxylic groups, of NH in NH2, and of CC in benzene rings, respectively. These absorption peaks are derived from the 2-aminoterephthalate ligand units in Cr-MOF.

Strong band similarity was observed between Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP and Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@NIP. The typical peaks between 438 cm−1 and 766 cm−1 are attributed to Si-stretching O's vibration, while the wide and strong absorption peak at 1064 cm−1 was a sign of typical asymmetric Si-O-Si stretching, pointing to the existence of SiO2 matrices. Additionally, the existence of the aminopropyl group of APTES was suggested by the stretching vibration bands of NH around 3381 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1 as well as the aliphatic CH stretching band at 2926 cm−1, which further indicated that the sol–gel polymerization reaction had effectively finished. The data showed that the Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP was effectively constructed.

The crystal structures of pure Cr-MOF and Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP were characterized by XRD. Fig. 2D shows that a Cr-MOF has a sharp diffraction peak located between 15 and 28˚. Furthermore, the intensity of the diffraction peak of Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIPs and Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@NIPs was significantly decreased compared with that of B-CuNCs@ZIF-8. Nevertheless, the spectra displayed one broad characteristic diffraction peak (20-30˚) of amorphous SiO2, as supported in literature [96,101].

The morphology and crystalline structure of Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP were characterized by FE-SEM. Fig. 3 a and b show that the shape of the dual emitters, Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP, was spherical, with polydisperse average sizes ranging from 25 nm to 788 nm. Elemental mapping using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) (Fig. 3C–I) reveal that all atoms, N, O, C, Si, and Cr were all present and uniformly distributed across the material area, which further demonstrated the Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP was successfully fabricated. The EDS spectra for Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP are shown in Fig. 3J. More images of FE-SEM for Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP are shown in Fig. S3 in the supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric analysis and study of interferences
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence emission of Y-CDs was quenched in the presence of ascorbic acid but not of Cr-MOF, and the fluorescence emission of Cr-MOF was quenched in the presence of uric acid, while that of Y-CDs remained constant. This can be exploited to design a dual-target analysis at the same time. Both emitters, Y-CDs and Cr-MOF, were embedded into the MIP to finally obtain an MIP with dual recognition sites.

To evaluate the improved selectivity of the dual imprinted sensor, the fluorescence response of precursors Y-CDs, Cr-MOF, and Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP towards AA, UA, and other ions and molecules was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence quenching response of the Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP by AA was much higher than that of other biological molecules, like urea, tartaric acid, glucose, lactose, and sucrose. Also, as shown in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence quenching response of Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP by UA was much higher than that of metal ions such as Cu2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Mg2+ and Cd2+. While the fluorescence emission of Y-CDs and Cr-MOF was quenched by most metals ions and biomolecules, as shown in Fig. 4A and 4B, the probe Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP was unaffected.. This means our integrated dual MIP-based probe, Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP, dramatically improved the selectivity towards AA and UA simultaneously due to the imprinting of two various cavities on the surface of MOF.

In order to obtain the best sensitivity from the probe, the pH of the working solution should be optimized. The fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP at different pH values in the range of 1.0–14.0 was studied. As displayed in Fig. 4C, emission of Cr-MOF (λem 450 nm) and Y-CDs (λem 560 nm) reached their maximum value when pH was between 5.0 and 9.0. The color changed and the fluorescence emission was quenched at low pH levels ranging from 1.0 to 4.0. Fluorescence decreased with increasing pH, which is due to the SiO2 layer being ionized and surface defects occurring at high pH[102]. The stability of the whole nanocomposite, Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP, was also studied and shown to be stable for more than two weeks.

In this study, the proposed sensor was studied for the detection of AA and UA both separately and simultaneously using Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP decreases in the presence of UA (in the absence of AA) due to the response of blue emissive Cr-MOF (Fig. 4D). In this case, the yellow emission of Y-CDs in the probe remained constant, as Y-CDs are sensitive to ascorbic acid. The fluorescence intensity of F450 exhibits a linear fluorescence relationship for UA in the concentration range of 25–425 µM with a coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.9950 (Fig. 5D). The calculated limit of detection (LOD) was 1.1 µM (when signal/noise = 3). Fig. 5B shows a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs in the Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP nanocomposite in the presence of AA (in the absence of UA). Fig. 5E shows that the fluorescence intensity of F560 exhibits a linear fluorescence relationship for AA in the concentrations range of 25–425 µM with a coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.996, and the calculated LOD was 1.3 µM. However, in the presence of the two targets, the blue and yellow fluorescence emission of the probe of Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP decreased (Fig. 5C). The fluorescence intensity of F450 exhibits a linear fluorescence relationship for UA determination in the concentrations range of 25 to 425 µM with a coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.995 (Fig. 5F); also, the fluorescence spectra of F560 exhibit a linear fluorescence relationship for AA detection in the same concentration range as UA (25 to 425 µM) with a coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.997 (Fig. 5F). This means that the presence of AA does not affect the linearity of the detection on UA, and vice versa.

In addition, the fluorescence spectra of non-imprinted nanocomposite Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@NIP were recorded (Fig. S2A). The fluorescence emission of Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@NIP does not quench dramatically compared to Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP due to the high affinity of the template cavities made in the MIP. Therefore, the calibration plot of Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@NIP is shown in Fig. S2B, and the coefficient of determination (R2) 0.993 and 0.992 for AA and UA, respectively.

To investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the Stern-Volmer equation was used (F0 /F = 1 + Ksv [Q]). The linearity of the Stern-Volmer plot (F0/F) vs quencher concentration ([Q]) implies that one sort of quenching process predominates. Positive deviations from linearity in Stern-Volmer plots show that the quenching process follows two processes at the same time, one of which is linked to dynamic quenching, and the other to static quenching. Static quenching is caused by the creation of a nonfluorescent complex between the fluorophore and the quencher in the ground state, or by the existence of a quenching sphere, which reduces the percentage of fluorescing molecules [103,104]. Fig. 6 A and B show a non-linearity plot for the two targets due to the mechanism quenching, indicating static and dynamic quenching.

The application of the Y-CDs/Cr-MOF@MIP probe for detection of AA and UA in body fluids, such as human urine was performed. The spike recoveries for quantitative determination of AA and UA in urine were carried out by adding different concentrations of AA and UA (150.0, 250.0, and 350.0 µM). The results are shown in Table 1 and show that the AA recoveries ranged from 82.48 % to 88.05 % for the UA and from 81.24 % to 85.73 % for the urine sample. The data indicated the applicability of the proposed dual target determinations based on one kind of MIPs in the body fluid. Table 2 shows a comparison of the reported methods in the literature with our proposed probe. As shown in the table, our nanocomposite biosensor has a reliable LOD and dynamic linearity range.

a simple and selective dual-target optical-based sensor was successfully developed for simultaneous detection of UA and AA based on surface imprinted MIPs on emissive Cr-MOF functionalized with Y-CDs. Due to the well-resolved emission peaks of the probes, one can successfully detect both targets at the same time. Spiked recoveries showed that the proposed dual-target MIP@MOP probe can be used for the detection of UA and AA in human urine samples. Our dual-target biosensor showed that it was sensitive (LOD as small as 1.1 μM and 1.3 µM for UA and AA, respectively), selective, and stable. Finally, the successfulAA and UA detection in human urine strongly demonstrates the possible real application of our dual-target MIP@MOF biosensor. Low-cost precursors and the robustness of the biosensor recommend using the probe for point of care testing (POCT).

Shano M. Pirot: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Writing – original draft. Khalid M. Omer: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration. Azad H. Alshatteri: Formal analysis. Gona K. Ali: Conceptualization, Validation. Omer B.A. Shatery: Preparation of the Cr-MOF.
